text: Welcome to another episode of robotic hernia surgery. Today we're actually going to be talking about management of diastasis recti with retro rectus repairs.

This was a fifty two year old man with a history of prior open epigastric hernia who presented with a six and a half centimeter diastasis of a small umbilical hernia. I'd like to highlight to everyone the EHS guidelines on management of rectus diastasis and the classification system, this patient fell into a T-II, D-III, H-I classification.

For these larger diastases that are symptomatic, I think it's important to have a thorough conversation with patients about strategies to repair not only the hernia, but potentially address the diastasis. The recommendations and guidelines are relatively weak with low-quality evidence to support the recommendations.

But in general, patients with midline hernias and diastasis should be managed with a mesh-based repair, especially larger diastasis. Options include onlay or retromuscular repairs. My preference is retromuscular for patients with obesity. This patient had a BMI of roughly

The surgeon demonstrates optical entry into the retromuscular space for an ETEP procedure. The rectus muscle is visible as insufflation is initiated through the first entry trocar. The retro rectus areolar plane is accessed, followed by dissection with a cannula, and placement of all ports for robotic operation.

The surgeon performs a crossover maneuver in the retromuscular space, taking advantage of the minimal previous operative interventions and the presence of diastasis. The procedure is conducted with increased aggressiveness due to the low risk of peritoneal violation and visceral adhesions.

The surgeon applies traction on the tissue to develop the preperitoneal dissection towards the contralateral posterior sheath, navigating the anatomical structures involved in the crossover procedure.
The surgeon develops the space within the abdominal cavity, revealing sutures from a prior epigastric hernia repair located centrally within the diastasis.
The surgeon performs a crossover dissection, aiming to create a broad space. An incision is made through the linea alba, mistakenly entering a medial area instead of the intended retro-rectus space.

When you cross over, I think staying central to where your eventual mesh will sit is a safe spot to guess or work towards identification of the retroactive space because if you're wrong, you will have a lot of mesh coverage. if you do that same error at the cephalad component of this you may have to extend your dissection beyond your intended dissection zone because now you'll need further mesh coverage but a central small defect into the Linea alba is not a major issue.
The surgeon develops the contralateral retro-rectus space and proceeds with the reduction of the umbilical hernia, which contains chronically incarcerated preperitoneal fat.

If this was the patient's only issue with their abdominal wall and they had presented with exclusively umbilical symptoms and no issues related to their diastasis, I have in those scenarios performed umbilical hernia repairs with a tap approach and then just extended my mesh coverage to the diastasis but not necessarily placated. This particular patient had a very symptomatic diastasis. We had a very thorough discussion. And so that is why we made this decision to proceed with an ETEP approach, a much more invasive approach than a traditional TAP umbilical hernia repair.

The surgeon reduces the herniated preperitoneal fat and performs limited preperitoneal dissection to separate the peritoneum from the posterior sheath. The diastasis has been addressed, and the retrorectus space is further developed to facilitate broad mesh coverage along the midline.

The surgeon observes the patient's broad rectus width, measuring approximately ten to eleven centimeters on each side. The anterior fascial closure is initiated below the hernia and diastasis, with placation and reinforcement of the fascia.
The surgeon uses a permanent number one V-lock suture, measuring eighteen inches, to address the area around the umbilical hernia.

The surgeon anchors the suture with three or four stitches to manage redundancy before sequentially tightening. The initial tightening reduces redundancy without drawing tissues closer together. Multiple sutures are used to placate the diastasis.

The surgeon performs sequential tightening during placation, running sutures back over themselves to draw the tissue together. Bites are taken between existing ones to avoid creating too many holes in the fascia. After placation, the focus shifts to mesh placement.
A 24 by 30 centimeter mesh is placed over the surgical area, and Tisseel is sprayed to secure it in place, marking the completion of the procedure.
